does: This paper challenges the standard assumption in reinforcement learning from human feedback that human preferences over trajectory segments depend only on partial return—the cumulative reward along each segment. The authors propose an alternative model where human preferences reflect regret, measuring how far a segment deviates from optimal behavior. They provide theoretical identifiability proofs showing that a reward function can be uniquely recovered from infinite regret-based preferences, while demonstrating that partial return-based models lack this identifiability in multiple settings. Empirically, they show their regret preference model outperforms the partial return model when learning from finite preference data.
connects: This work builds directly on the reinforcement learning from human feedback literature, which has become central to aligning large language models and RL agents with human values. Prior work typically modeled human preferences using the Bradley-Terry model applied to trajectory returns, assuming preferences depend only on cumulative rewards. This paper extends foundational inverse reinforcement learning research questioning what aspects of behavior humans actually evaluate, while connecting to recent critiques of return-based preference modeling in RLHF. The identifiability analysis relates to classical work on reward function recovery in inverse RL, applying these theoretical tools to the preference-based setting.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more accurate human preference modeling in RLHF applications, potentially improving alignment of language models and RL systems with genuine human interests rather than spurious correlations with trajectory returns. The regret-based preference model could enable better preference elicitation methods that capture human judgments about decision quality rather than just outcomes. The identifiability results provide theoretical foundations for future work on what information human preferences must contain to uniquely specify reward functions, and suggest new experimental designs for collecting preferences that better reflect human evaluation criteria in practical alignment applications.
